谷歌的母公司Alphabet今天表示，它已经解决了一系列与公司处理性骚扰指控有关的股东诉讼。Alphabet将在未来十年承诺向企业多元化项目投入3.1亿美元，该公司同意允许董事会在不当行为案件中发挥更大的监督作用。
谷歌人力资源运营副总裁艾琳·诺顿在一篇公司博客文章中表示，作为新协议的一部分，Alphabet将扩大其目前的政策，禁止任何因任何形式的不当行为而被解雇的人获得遣散费，包括目前因性行为不当或报复而接受调查的任何人。
这项和解协议是2018年投资者团体在加州对Alphabet提起的一系列合并诉讼的结果，这些诉讼指控Alphabet在可信地指控其性骚扰时，留住了男性高管，并支付了高额款项，违反了对股东的受托责任。(据《纽约时报》报道，联邦法院和特拉华州的其他股东诉讼仍在进行中。)。
在这些诉讼之前，有报道称，谷歌三名高管因被指控行为不当而离开公司，他们带着巨额补偿方案悄悄离开了公司。2014年，Android创始人安迪·鲁宾(Andy Rubin)在谷歌认定针对他的性行为不当指控可信后，被带出公司，获得了1.5亿美元的股票奖励和9000万美元的遣散费。
2018年，数千名谷歌员工举行罢工，抗议公司处理鲁宾和其他人案件的方式。抗议组织者要求谷歌在有关骚扰和薪酬不平等的报告上提高透明度，并要求安全匿名举报不当行为的新程序，以及结束强制仲裁。
作为和解协议的一部分，仲裁现在将是可选的，而不是强制性的，不仅适用于Alphabet的全职员工，也适用于与Alphabet存在骚扰、歧视或报复纠纷的临时员工、供应商和独立承包商-这是该公司2018年承诺的扩大，即在性骚扰案件中不要求仲裁。员工也不会被要求同意保密协议，以了解性骚扰或报复的指控。
Alphabet还承诺加强调查流程，包括我们如何升级担忧、多快做出回应、如何重新审理案件等等。涉及公司高级领导层的投诉也将被指派一个专家团队，向董事会报告结果。
谷歌还将正式禁止直接报告或管理链中的人际关系(浪漫、肉体、家庭关系)，而不是仅仅强烈劝阻他们，这显然是公司的政策，直到目前为止，这一直是公司的政策。在这一点上，谷歌将正式禁止直接报告或管理链中的人际关系，而不仅仅是强烈反对这些关系，这显然是公司的政策。
诺顿说，[首席执行官]桑达尔[皮查伊]、[多样性、公平和包容性]咨询委员会以及董事会将共同坚持Alphabet的坚定承诺，禁止和有效回应有关性骚扰、歧视和报复的投诉，并促进工作场所的多样性、公平和包容性。我感谢每个人，特别是我们的员工和股东，他们为我们提供了反馈，并确保我们今天解决这些重要问题的方式比过去更好。
note_en:
Alphabet, Google's parent company, said today it has settled a set of shareholder lawsuits related to the company's handling of sexual harassment claims. Alphabet will commit $310 million to corporate diversity programs over the next decade, and the company agreed to allow its board to take on a greater oversight role in misconduct cases.
 As part of the new agreement, Alphabet will expand on its current policy of "prohibiting severance for anyone terminated for any form of misconduct," to include anyone who is currently under investigation for "sexual misconduct or retaliation," Google VP of People Operations Eileen Naughton said in a  company blog post.
 The settlement is the outcome of a consolidated set of lawsuits investor groups  filed against Alphabet in California in 2018, alleging that the company breached its fiduciary duty to shareholders when it retained, and handsomely paid off, male executives credibly accused of sexual harassment. (Other shareholder suits, in federal court and in Delaware, are still in progress, according to the  New York Times.)
 The lawsuits followed  reports finding that three top Google executives who left the company amid allegations of misconduct got to leave quietly with  massive compensation packages. Android creator Andy Rubin was given a $150 million stock grant, as well as a $90 million severance package, when he was ushered out of the company in 2014 after Google determined a sexual misconduct complaint against him was credible.
 Thousands of Google employees  staged a walkout in 2018 in protest over the way the company handled the cases of Rubin and others. Protest organizers demanded more transparency from Google over reports of harassment and pay inequity, as well as a new process for safely and anonymously reporting misconduct and an end to forced arbitration.
 As part of the settlement, arbitration will now be optional, rather than mandatory, not only for full-time Alphabet employees but also for "temporary staff, vendors, and independent contractors" who have harassment, discrimination, or retaliation disputes with Alphabet—an expansion of its  2018 pledge not to require arbitration in sexual harassment cases. Employees will also not be required to agree to nondisclosure agreements to settle claims of sexual harassment or retaliation.
 Alphabet also promised to "enhance investigations processes," including "how we escalate concerns, how quickly we respond, how we reopen cases, and more." Complaints involving company senior leadership will also have a "specialist team" assigned, which will report its results to the board of directors.
 Google will also officially prohibit personal relationships ("romantic, physical, familial") in direct reporting or management chains, instead of just "strongly discouraging" them, which was apparently company policy until this point.
 "Together, [CEO] Sundar [Pichai], the [Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion] Advisory Council, and the Board will uphold Alphabet’s unwavering commitment to prohibit and respond effectively to complaints of sexual harassment, discrimination, and retaliation and promote diversity, equity, and inclusion in the workplace," Naughton said. "’m grateful to everyone, especially our employees and shareholders, for providing us with feedback, and for making sure that the way we tackle these vital issues is better today than it was in the past."
title: 谷歌就处理骚扰指控的股东诉讼达成和解
title_en: Google settles shareholder lawsuit over handling of harassment claims

note:
本周，我们来看看“主/从”术语在计算机编程中的使用，以及加州零工工人的现状。
“人力资本”将很快以每周时事通讯的形式推出。你可以在这里报名。
这对开发人员来说可能不是什么新闻，但当我发现许多科技公司仍然在使用奴隶-主人语言时，这对我来说是个新闻。现在，微软旗下的GitHub正在准备删除这些对奴隶制的引用，将主代码库命名为“main”而不是“master”。这些变化将于10月1日起生效。
GitHub早在6月份就谈到了做出这些改变，当时首席执行官纳特·弗里德曼(Nat Friedman)在推特上表示，这是公司已经在努力的事情。但GitHub绝不是第一家考虑并做出这些改变的公司。2014年，开源平台Drupal将“主/从”改为“主/副本”。
它做出改变的原因之一是，“奴隶”这个词有负面的含义(尽管这可能与一个术语的命名相关，也可能与之无关)，包括多世纪的奴隶制历史，以造福欧洲殖民列强，今天的监狱劳工有时被迫在类似奴隶制的条件下工作，今天世界许多地方的年轻女孩被贩卖为性奴隶。“。
然后，在2018年，编程语言Python抛弃了种族主义术语。与此同时，据CNET报道，Twitter今年早些时候开始采取措施替换这些术语，并希望在2021年底之前完成这些术语的替换。
令人疯狂的是，这些术语从一开始就存在，现在才开始讨论。早在2003年，洛杉矶市政府官员就要求其制造商和供应商停止使用这些术语，但他们并没有要求这样做。
因此，也许难怪一些科技公司难以留住黑人员工。例如，2019年，谷歌报告称，其黑人和拉丁裔人才的流失率-这表明员工每年的离职率-高于全国平均水平。当种族主义融入一家公司的技术框架时，它就会延续白人比黑人优越的错误观念。
与零工相关的两件大事正在发生：22号提案(Prop 22)，这是加州由优步(Uber)、Lyft、Insta和DoorDash支持的法案，旨在将工人归类为独立承包商，以及植根于AB 5的诉讼。AB 5是加州今年早些时候生效的法律，规定了如何正确归类零工。
让我们从22号提案开始吧。加州大学伯克利分校政府研究所的一项新民意调查发现，这将是一场势均力敌的选举。加州大学伯克利分校的IGS对5900名可能的选民进行了调查，发现39%的选民会对第22号提案投赞成票，36%的人表示会投反对票。其余25%的人尚未做出决定。
正如我们在上星期所说，支持22号的阵营已投入约1.8亿元，而22号反对阵营的阵营则投入约460万元。与此同时，我们在22个点播应用中看到了Yes的广告。
在AB 5方面，优步和Lyft仍在法庭上，此前加利福尼亚州总检察长泽维尔·贝塞拉(Xavier Becera)以及来自洛杉矶、圣地亚哥和旧金山的市政律师起诉了这两家公司，指控它们对工人进行错误分类。上诉法院批准暂缓执行初步禁令，该禁令将迫使优步(Uber)和Lyft立即对司机进行重新分类。上诉法院已经提交了一些之友案情摘要。
在美国国家就业法小组、美国公民自由联盟和其他民权组织提交的一份简报中，他们表示，优步和Lyft将有色人种工人归类为独立承包商，从而伤害了他们。
许多贫穷的有色人种工人和移民被困在一个工资过低的不平等经济中，在工作中处于危险之中，无法获得带薪病假、失业保险、工伤补偿和其他保护，只能自食其力。通过坚称司机不是员工，Lyft和Uber进一步疏远了有色人种员工与提供真正灵活性和经济安全的基础工作场所权利的距离。相反，他们的商业模式将贫困工人困在贫困和经济排斥的棘手循环中。
本月早些时候，优步(Uber)和Lyft首席执行官洛根·格林(Logan Green)都提交了宣誓声明，证实他们都计划遵守一项要求他们对各自员工进行重新分类的命令。如果优步和Lyft被迫对司机进行重新分类，优步首席执行官Dara Khosrowshahi和Lyft首席执行官洛根·格林(Logan Green)都提交了宣誓声明。
在Khosrowshahi的声明中，他只是简单地说，“优步已经制定了实施计划”，以在不超过30天的时间内遵守一项命令。在格林的声明中，他说，“这样的实施可能包括停止加州全部或部分地区的拼车业务。”
note_en:
This week, we’re looking at the use of “master/slave” terminology in computer programming and the current state of gig workers in California.
 Human Capital will soon be available as a weekly newsletter. You can  sign up here.
     This probably isn’t news to developers, but it was news to me when I found out many tech companies still use slave-master language. Now,   Microsoft-owned GitHub is gearing up to remove these references  to slavery by naming primary code repositories “main” instead of “master.” These changes will go into effect on October 1.
 GitHub talked about making these changes as early as June, when   CEO Nat Friedman tweeted  that it was something the company was already working on. But GitHub is by no means the first company to consider and make these changes.   In 2014 , open source platform Drupal moved to replace “master/slave” with “primary/replica.”
 One of its reasons for making the change was, “The word “slave” has negative connotations (although this might or might not be relevant in the naming of a technical term) including multi-century history of slavery to benefit European colonial powers, prison laborers today forced to work in conditions at times resembling that slavery, young girls sold into sex slavery in many parts of the world today.”
 Then, in 2018,   programming language python ditched the racist terminology . Meanwhile,   Twitter began taking steps to replace those terms earlier this year  and hopes to finish replacing that terminology by the end of 2021,   according to CNET .
 What’s wild is that these terms ever existed in the first place and are just now being addressed.   While Los Angeles city officials way back in 2003 asked its  manufacturers and suppliers to stop using the terminology, they did not require it.
 So perhaps it’s no wonder why some tech companies struggle to retain Black employees. In 2019, for example,  Google reported its attrition rates of Black and Latinx talent — which indicate the rate at which employees leave on an annual basis — were higher than the national average. When racism is built into the technical framework of a company, it perpetuates a false idea that white people are superior to Black people.
     Two big things are happening pertaining to gig workers: Prop 22, the California bill backed by Uber, Lyft, Instacart and DoorDash that seeks to keep workers classified as independent contractors and lawsuits rooted in AB 5, the California law that went into effect earlier this year that lays out how to properly classify gig workers.
 Let’s start with Prop 22.  A new poll from the UC Berkeley Institute of Governmental Studies found that it’s going to be a close election. In a survey of 5,900 likely voters, UC Berkeley’s IGS found that 39% of voters would vote yes on Prop 22 while 36% said they would vote no. The other 25% are undecided.
 As we mentioned last week, the Yes on 22 campaign has put in about $180 million into the campaign while the No on 22 side has put in about $4.6 million. Meanwhile, we’re seeing ads for Yes on 22 inside on-demand apps.
  On the AB 5 side of things, Uber and Lyft are still in court  after California Attorney General Xavier Becerra, along with city attorneys from Los Angeles, San Diego and San Francisco sued the companies, alleging they are misclassifying their workers. In the appeals court, which granted a stay on the preliminary injunction that would force Uber and Lyft to immediately reclassify their drivers, a number of amicus briefs have been filed.
 In a  brief filed by the National Employment Law Group, the ACLU and other civil rights groups, they say Uber and Lyft harm workers of color by classifying them as independent contractors.
 Many poor workers of color and immigrants are stuck in a separate and unequal economy where they are underpaid, put in harm’s way on the job, and left to fend for themselves without access to paid sick leave, unemployment insurance, workers’ compensation, and other protections. By insisting that their drivers are not employees, Lyft and Uber further distance workers of colors from the bedrock workplace rights that provide real flexibility and economic security. Instead, their business models trap poor workers into intractable cycles of poverty and economic exclusion.
 In the event Uber and Lyft are forced to reclassify their drivers, both Uber CEO Dara Khosrowshahi and Lyft CEO Logan Green filed sworn statements earlier this month that confirmed they both have plans to comply with an order requiring them to reclassify their respective workforces.
 In Khosrowshahi’s statement, he simply said “Uber has developed implementation plans” to comply with an order within no more than 30 days. In Green’s statement, he said “such an implementation may include ceasing rideshare operations in all or some parts of California.”
title: 人力资本：摆脱“主/从”术语
title_en: Human Capital: Moving away from ‘master/slave’ terminology

note:
2017年，苹果的两位顶尖设计和技术高管决定离开公司，怀着一个雄心勃勃的愿景开始自己的创业：创造下一个大型计算范式。这家名为Humane的初创公司现在宣布，它已经在首轮风险投资中筹集了3000万美元，以继续开发其神秘的产品。虽然联合创始人伊姆兰·乔杜里(Imran Chaudhri)和贝瑟尼·邦乔诺(Bethany Bongiorno)拒绝透露他们正在做什么，但它的联合创始人似乎相信，它将产生与iPhone相同的影响-考虑到其中一人设计了iPhone的原始界面，iPhone的影响很大。
Humane总裁兼董事长乔杜里(Chaudhri)表示：“就计算平台而言，我们正处于一定程度的成熟，导致创新能力下降。”乔杜里在苹果工作了21年，致力于iPhone和iPad的用户界面设计。对我们来说，这真的是关于我们如何真正带来活力，以及我们如何为计算带来新的机遇。我们怎么移动针头呢？“。
在它至少揭示了一些细节之前，我们不可能对人类创造的潜在影响有什么看法。托尼·法德尔(Tony Fadell)是iPod的先驱，也帮助我们带来了iPhone。他在十多年前离开了苹果，不久之后就开始谈论“一款可能对大问题产生巨大影响的产品”，他在2011年告诉“纽约时报”(New York Times)。他的初创公司Nest的发明原来是一个恒温器。但Humane肯定提高了人们对它正在做的事情的期望。
这家初创公司的雄心在一定程度上源于乔杜里考虑到了他帮助创造的产品的缺点-特别是智能手机可以唠叨和垄断我们的注意力，同时窃取私人数据让企业获利。但这并不意味着乔杜里和曾与乔杜里合作推出iPad的苹果前软件工程总监邦乔尔诺认为，技术在社会上是一股净负面力量。
“我们本质上是技术乐观主义者，”Humane首席执行官邦乔尔诺(Bongiorno)说。“但我们认为，现在也是时候对一切提出质疑，以建造更好的东西了。”
相反，他们押注的是，现在是时候用一种新的、侵入性更小的方式与计算机交互了。这是一个令人信服的愿景，尽管缺乏细节。“每一种产品，特别是在计算领域，在它能走多远，在它开始真正变得筋疲力尽之前，它能做什么，都是有限制的。就创造力而言，这是耗尽的，在有用性方面是耗尽的，在使用体验方面也是耗尽的，“乔杜里说。“这是一件经常发生的周期性事件。从某种角度来看，有些人称之为15年周期。我们正在为智能手机做这件事。“。
虽然这两人原本计划在旧金山大流行封锁开始后的一周内筹集资金，但他们最终在6月份与投资者举行了会议。不到10天，他们就找到了下一个合作伙伴：Y Combinator前总裁、人工智能开发组织OpenAI首席执行官萨姆·阿尔特曼(Sam Altman)，以及企业家、天使投资者、Strike早期员工拉奇·格罗姆(Lachy Groom)。这两家投资者贡献了首轮融资的大部分资金，其余部分由马克·贝尼奥夫(Marc Benioff)、瓦利亚风险投资公司(Valia Ventures)、Plexo Capital和Kindred Ventures等其他投资者组成。
“我认为这个团队非常出色，他们对一个新的计算平台的愿景，以及这对整个世界意味着什么，再加上他们的专业知识，实现了像这样的超级雄心勃勃的事情。。。这是我很兴奋看到的事情，并立即想成为其中的一部分，“奥特曼说。
最终，等待将结束，我们其余的人将能够看到有什么值得大惊小怪的。Bongiorno表示，公司计划在明年年底前公布细节。但当我们交谈时，格洛姆提供了一个小小的提示：“这是那些事后才看起来很明显的事情之一，”他说。
note_en:
In 2017, two of Apple’s top design and technology executives decided to leave the company to start their own venture with an ambitious vision: to create the next big computing paradigm. That startup, called  Humane, is now announcing that it has raised $30 million in a series A round of venture funding to continue developing its mysterious product. While cofounders Imran Chaudhri and Bethany Bongiorno refuse to reveal what they’re working on, its cofounders seem to believe it will have the same kind of impact as the iPhone—which is saying a lot, given that one of them designed the iPhone’s original interface.
   “We are at a point in terms of computing platforms where there’s a certain level of maturation that’s led to a decline in innovation,” says Humane president and chairman Chaudhri, who worked on the UI design of the iPhone and the iPad during his 21 years at Apple. “For us, it’s really about how we actually bring vitality and how we bring a new opportunity to computing. How do we move the needle?”
 It’s impossible to have an opinion about the potential impact of Humane’s creation until it reveals at least some details. Tony Fadell, who spearheaded the iPod and also helped bring us the iPhone, left Apple over a decade ago and soon after started talking about “a product that could have a huge impact on a big problem,” as he told   The  New York Times in 2011. His startup Nest’s creation turned out to be a thermostat. But Humane is certainly dialing up the expectations for whatever it’s working on.
 The startup’s ambitions derive in part from Chaudhri  reckoning with the downsides of the products he was instrumental in creating—particularly the ways in which smartphones can nag at and monopolize our attention while siphoning away private data for corporations to profit from. But that doesn’t mean that Chaudhri and Bongiorno, a former director of software engineering at Apple who worked with Chaudhri to launch the iPad, believe that technology is a net negative force in society.
 “We’re tech optimists at heart,” says Bongiorno, who is Humane’s CEO. “But we believe it’s also time to question everything to build something better.”
 Instead, they’re betting that the time is right for a new, less invasive way to interface with computers. It’s a compelling vision, despite the lack of details. “Every product, especially in computing, has a limitation in terms of how far it can go, what it can do before it starts to really become exhausted. That’s exhausted in terms of creativity, exhausted in terms of usefulness, exhausted in terms of the experience of using it as well,” Chaudhri says. “This is a cyclical thing that’s always occurred. Some people from a certain perspective call it a 15-year cycle. We’re coming on that for the smartphone.”
 While the duo originally planned to raise funds the week after the pandemic lockdown began in San Francisco, they ultimately ended up taking meetings with investors in June. Within 10 days, they had found their next partners: Sam Altman, former president of Y Combinator and the CEO of AI-development organization OpenAI, and Lachy Groom, an entrepreneur, angel investor, and early employee at Stripe. These two investors are contributing most of the series A round, while a small syndicate of other investors, including  Marc Benioff, Valia Ventures, Plexo Capital, and Kindred Ventures, make up the rest.
  “I thought the team was exceptional and their vision for a new computing platform and what that would mean for the world as a whole, combined with their expertise delivering on super ambitious things like this . . . it was something I was excited to see and immediately wanted to be part of,” Altman says.
 Eventually, the wait will be over and the rest of us will be able to see what the fuss is all about. Bongiorno says the company plans to announce the details before the end of next year. But when we spoke, Groom provided a small hint: “It was one of those things that only seems obvious in hindsight,” he says.
title: Humanity筹集了3000万美元的首轮融资，由Sam Altman和Lachy Groom牵头，马克·贝尼奥夫(Marc Benioff)和其他人参与其中，以建立某种新型的计算平台
title_en: Humane raises a $30M Series A led by Sam Altman and Lachy Groom, with Marc Benioff and others participating, to build some new kind of computing platform
